one_line_takeaway: InvLBA shows that moving clean-label backdoor triggers from pixel space to the Stable Diffusion latent space (via a learned, end-to-end trainable residual perturbation at an intermediate denoising step) fixes the sharp ASR degradation that existing clean-label attacks suffer when applied to generative-data-augmentation pipelines, raising average ASR by 46.43 points over baselines while staying imperceptible to human inspection and evading Neural Cleanse and largely surviving DATAELIXIR purification.
key_finding: InvLBA raises attack success rate (ASR) by 46.43 percentage points on average over the strongest per-dataset baseline setting in Table 2, while keeping clean-accuracy drop (CAD) near zero (average CAD +0.03%). Per-dataset ASR for InvLBA: CIFAR-10-S 98.27%, ImageNet-10-S 72.44%, CelebA-S 97.56%, Caltech-101 53.15%, Cars 39.91%, Pets 69.12% (average 71.74%), versus the best baseline+GDA results which peaked around 75.20% only on CelebA-S via Narcissus but were far lower elsewhere (e.g., COMBAT+GDA ASR only 14.89% on ImageNet-10-S, 4.40% average). The paper also shows the theoretical motivation empirically: Table 1 reports APS (MDc) rising from 0.33 (original data) to 0.45 (generated data, existing attacks) on ImageNet-10-S, and from 0.29 to 0.30 on Pets, while InvLBA's own trigger achieves lower APS on generated data (0.38 and 0.23 respectively) than existing attacks on generated data, consistent with the theory that lower APS tightens the poison-generalization-error bound. Cross-architecture transfer (Table 3, ResNet-18 surrogate) mostly keeps ASR above 70% on unseen victim backbones (MobileNetV2, VGG13, ViT-Small-8), with one exception: ViT-Small-8 on ImageNet-10-S drops to 50%. Under DATAELIXIR purification (Table 4), ASR still remains above 50% on all three tested datasets (drops of 19.99, 21.24, 16.06 points from 98.27/72.44/97.56 baseline). Against Neural Cleanse, the method's anomaly index stays below the detection threshold of 2 on all three datasets. In a 20-participant human inspection test (Table 5) using the WaNet-style protocol (1 poisoned image among 5), InvLBA achieves an average success fooling rate (SFR) of 80.22%, statistically indistinguishable from the 80% expected under random guessing, indicating the trigger is imperceptible even to annotators primed to look for backdoor patterns.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) a theoretical analysis, building on a generalization bound from Yu et al. (2024), showing that existing pixel-level clean-label backdoor triggers fail on generative-data-augmentation (GDA) pipelines because a term the authors call Adversarial Perturbation Sensitivity (APS) is higher on generated images than on natural images, loosening the poison-generalization-error upper bound; (2) InvLBA, a new invisible clean-label backdoor attack that injects a fixed, learned trigger (a multiplicative/additive residual transform, z'_t = (1+w)z_t + b) directly into the latent space of a Stable Diffusion v1.4 model during an intermediate denoising step, using a one-step noise-prediction trick to make the whole pipeline end-to-end trainable against a frozen surrogate classifier, plus a rho-ball L-infinity projection on the perturbed latent to bound trigger magnitude; and (3) new theorems (Thm 5.1, with Remarks 5.2/5.3) giving generalization guarantees for both clean accuracy and attack success rate of InvLBA, plus an empirical validation across 6 datasets, cross-architecture transfer, two SOTA defenses, and a 20-participant human inspection study.
threat_model: Attacker poisons the generated-data side of a GDA pipeline: given an original labeled dataset D_o and a GDA generator G (e.g., DistDiff built on Stable Diffusion v1.4), the attacker controls/injects a subset of target-class generated images with an imperceptible latent-space trigger while leaving all labels unchanged (clean-label attack), so poisoned samples pass human/label inspection. A victim model is then trained by a downstream user on the union of original data, poisoned generated data, and clean generated data. At inference, the victim model behaves normally on clean inputs but predicts the attacker's target class c when the trigger is present. The paper considers both a white-box setting (surrogate model architecture = victim model) and a black-box/transfer setting (surrogate differs from the downstream victim backbone: MobileNetV2, VGG13, ViT-Small-8). It also evaluates against one data-level defense (DATAELIXIR, diffusion-based purification) and one model-level defense (Neural Cleanse) plus a human-inspection study, i.e., the defender's assumed capabilities are: filtering/purifying poisoned images before training, or reverse-engineering triggers from a trained model, or visual inspection by humans.
limitations: The paper itself notes ASR is markedly lower on datasets with many classes (39.91% on Cars, 196 classes; 53.15% on Caltech-101, 102 classes), attributing this to targeted attacks being inherently harder with more classes, and flags improving effectiveness on high-class-count datasets as future work. It also explicitly calls for future work on defenses against backdoor attacks targeting generated data, implicitly acknowledging no defense fully mitigates InvLBA in this paper's experiments (DATAELIXIR reduces but does not eliminate ASR; Neural Cleanse fails to flag it at all). All experiments use small-scale GDA settings (100 images/class or comparable), Stable Diffusion v1.4 specifically with DistDiff as the sole GDA method tested, and a fixed poison rate (20%) and target class (c=0) throughout the main results, so generalization to other GDA pipelines, generators, or poison rates is not empirically demonstrated in the paper.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10-S (100 images/class subset of CIFAR-10, 10 classes, 1000 samples)
ImageNet-10-S (100 images/class subset of 10 randomly selected ImageNet-1K classes, 1000 samples)
CelebA-S (100 images/class subset of CelebA, 8 classes, 800 samples)
quantitative_results: InvLBA per-dataset ASR: CIFAR-10-S 98.27%, ImageNet-10-S 72.44%, CelebA-S 97.56%, Caltech-101 53.15%, Cars 39.91%, Pets 69.12%

Average ASR improvement over best-performing baseline setting per dataset: +46.43 percentage points (dataset-level gains: +85.76 CIFAR-10-S, +31.55 ImageNet-10-S, +22.36 CelebA-S, +33.62 Caltech-101, +18.55 Cars, +36.68 Pets)

Cross-architecture transfer ASR (Table 3, ResNet-18 surrogate): mostly >70% on MobileNetV2/VGG13/ViT-Small-8 across CIFAR-10-S, ImageNet-10-S, CelebA-S; lowest case ViT-Small-8 on ImageNet-10-S at 50.00%

DATAELIXIR defense (Table 4): ASR drops from 98.27/72.44/97.56 to 78.28/51.20/81.50 on CIFAR-10-S/ImageNet-10-S/CelebA-S respectively (deltas of 19.99/21.24/16.06 points), remaining above 50% in all cases
Neural Cleanse anomaly index stays below the 2.0 detection threshold for InvLBA on CIFAR-10-S, ImageNet-10-S, CelebA-S

Human inspection (Table 5): average Success Fooling Rate (SFR) 80.22% over CIFAR-10-S (80.78%), ImageNet-10-S (80.43%), Cars (79.45%), vs. 80% expected under random guessing (1-in-5), from 200 groups per dataset judged by 20 participants
COMBAT (baseline) average ASR drop of 14.84% when combined with GDA vs. without, illustrating the paper's motivating problem
baselines_compared: BadNets (Gu et al.) adapted to clean-label setting, 5x5 trigger
LC / Label-Consistent backdoor attack (Turner et al.)
Sleeper Agent (Souri et al., single-source setting)
Narcissus (Zeng et al., 5 warm-up rounds, L-infinity budget 8/255)
Each baseline evaluated both without GDA and '+GDA' (baseline trigger applied to generated images)
